---
title: "Class_09"
author: "Matthew Chan (A18130675)"
format: pdf
---
## 1 Background
Objective: Your task is to explore their candy dataset to find out answers to these types of questions - but most of all your job is to have fun, learn by doing hands on data analysis, and hopefully make this type of analysis less frightening for the future! Let’s get started.

## 2 Importing candy data


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
candyfile <- "candy-data.csv"
candy = read.csv(candyfile, row.names=1)
head(candy)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
             chocolate fruity caramel peanutyalmondy nougat crispedricewafer
100 Grand            1      0       1              0      0                1
3 Musketeers         1      0       0              0      1                0
One dime             0      0       0              0      0                0
One quarter          0      0       0              0      0                0
Air Heads            0      1       0              0      0                0
Almond Joy           1      0       0              1      0                0
             hard bar pluribus sugarpercent pricepercent winpercent
100 Grand       0   1        0        0.732        0.860   66.97173
3 Musketeers    0   1        0        0.604        0.511   67.60294
One dime        0   0        0        0.011        0.116   32.26109
One quarter     0   0        0        0.011        0.511   46.11650
Air Heads       0   0        0        0.906        0.511   52.34146
Almond Joy      0   1        0        0.465        0.767   50.34755
```


:::
:::


# 2.1 What is in the dataset

>Q1. How many different candy types are in this dataset?

There are 85 rows in this dataset


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
nrow(candy)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 85
```


:::
:::


>Q2. How many fruity candy types are in the dataset?

There are 38 fruity candy types in this dataset


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
sum(candy$fruity)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 38
```


:::
:::


# 2.2 What is your favorite candy?

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(dplyr)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```

Attaching package: 'dplyr'
```

```
The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':

    filter, lag
```



:::

```{.r .cell-code}
candy |> 
  filter(row.names(candy)=="") |> 
  select(winpercent)
```

```

<0 rows> (or 0-length row.names)
```


:::
:::


>Q3. What is your favorite candy (other than Twix) in the dataset and what is it’s winpercent value?

Haribo gold bears have a winpercent of 57.11974%


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
candy |> 
  filter(row.names(candy)=="Haribo Gold Bears") |> 
  select(winpercent)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                  winpercent
Haribo Gold Bears   57.11974
```


:::
:::


>Q4. What is the winpercent value for “Kit Kat”?

Kit Kat have a winpercent of 76.7686%


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
candy |> 
  filter(row.names(candy)=="Kit Kat") |> 
  select(winpercent)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
        winpercent
Kit Kat    76.7686
```


:::
:::


>Q5. What is the winpercent value for “Tootsie Roll Snack Bars”?

Tootsie Roll Snack Bars have a winpercent of 49.6535%


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
candy |> 
  filter(row.names(candy)=="Tootsie Roll Snack Bars") |> 
  select(winpercent)
```

```
                        winpercent
Tootsie Roll Snack Bars    49.6535
```

>Q6. Is there any variable/column that looks to be on a different scale to the majority of the other columns in the dataset?

The variable that looks to be on a different scale is winpercent.

>Q7. What do you think a zero and one represent for the candy$chocolate column?

A zero is for candy that is not chocolate and the one is for a candy considered a chocolate. 

## 3 Exploratory analysis
>Q8. Plot a histogram of winpercent values using both base R an ggplot2.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
hist(candy$winpercent, breaks=20)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-9-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggplot2)

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent) +
  geom_density(bins=12, fill= "lightblue", col="gray")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in geom_density(bins = 12, fill = "lightblue", col = "gray"): Ignoring
unknown parameters: `bins`
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-10-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::



> Q9. Is the distribution of winpercent values symmetrical?

No, it doesnt look symmetrical it seems skewed to the right.

> Q10. Is the center of the distribution above or below 50%?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy)+
  aes(winpercent) +
  geom_boxplot()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-11-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(candy$winpercent)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 50.31676
```


:::
:::


The center of the distribution is 50.3167638 and is above 50%

> Q11. On average is chocolate candy higher or lower ranked than fruit candy?

Steps to solve this: 
1. find all chocolate candy in the dataset 
2. extract or find their winpercent values
3. calculate the mean of these values
4. find all fruit candy in the dataset and repeat


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
choc.candy <- candy[candy$chocolate == 1,]
choc.win <- choc.candy$winpercent
mean(choc.win)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 60.92153
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fruit.candy <- candy[ candy$fruity == 1,]
fruit.win <- fruit.candy$winpercent
mean(fruit.win)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 44.11974
```


:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(candy$winpercent[candy$chocolate == 1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 60.92153
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
mean(candy$winpercent[candy$fruity == 1])
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 44.11974
```


:::
:::


Chocolate candy is higher ranked than fruity candy with a winrate of 60.9215294 to 44.1197414

> Q12. Is this difference statistically significant?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
t.test(choc.win, fruit.win)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```

	Welch Two Sample t-test

data:  choc.win and fruit.win
t = 6.2582, df = 68.882, p-value = 2.871e-08
alternative hypothesis: true difference in means is not equal to 0
95 percent confidence interval:
 11.44563 22.15795
sample estimates:
mean of x mean of y 
 60.92153  44.11974 
```


:::
:::

Yes, because the p-value is less than 0.05

## 4 Overall Candy Rankings

> Q15. Make a first barplot of candy ranking based on winpercent values.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, rownames(candy)) +
  geom_col()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-20-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


> Q16. This is quite ugly, use the reorder() function to get the bars sorted by winpercent?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, reorder(rownames(candy), winpercent)) +
  geom_col() + 
  ylab("") +
  theme_classic()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-21-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


# 4.0.1 Time to add some useful color

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
my_cols=rep("black", nrow(candy))
my_cols[as.logical(candy$chocolate)] = "chocolate"
my_cols[as.logical(candy$bar)] = "brown"
my_cols[as.logical(candy$fruity)] = "pink"

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, reorder(rownames(candy), winpercent)) +
  geom_col(fill=my_cols) +
  ylab("") +
  theme_classic()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-22-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


Now, for the first time, using this plot we can answer questions like:

> Q17. What is the worst ranked chocolate candy?

Nestle Smarties
> Q18. What is the best ranked fruity candy?

Starburst

## 5 Taking a look at pricepercent
We can use the **ggrepel** package for better label placement


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(candy) +
  aes(winpercent, pricepercent, label=rownames(candy)) +
  geom_point(col=my_cols) + 
  geom_text_repel(col=my_cols, size=3.3, max.overlaps = 5)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: ggrepel: 65 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-23-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


> Q19. Which candy type is the highest ranked in terms of winpercent for the least money - i.e. offers the most bang for your buck?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent, decreasing = FALSE)
head( candy[ord,c(11,12)], n=10 )
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                     pricepercent winpercent
Tootsie Roll Midgies        0.011   45.73675
Pixie Sticks                0.023   37.72234
Dum Dums                    0.034   39.46056
Fruit Chews                 0.034   43.08892
Strawberry bon bons         0.058   34.57899
Root Beer Barrels           0.069   29.70369
Sixlets                     0.081   34.72200
Hershey's Kisses            0.093   55.37545
Lemonhead                   0.104   39.14106
One dime                    0.116   32.26109
```


:::
:::

The candy type highest ranked in terms of winpercent for the least money is Tootsie Roll Midgies with 45.73% winpercent for 0.011 price percent or Hershey's Kisses for 55.37545% winpercent to 0.093 price percent.
> Q20. What are the top 5 most expensive candy types in the dataset and of these which is the least popular?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ord <- order(candy$pricepercent, decreasing = T)
head( candy[ord,c(11,12)], n=5 )
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                         pricepercent winpercent
Nik L Nip                       0.976   22.44534
Nestle Smarties                 0.976   37.88719
Ring pop                        0.965   35.29076
Hershey's Krackel               0.918   62.28448
Hershey's Milk Chocolate        0.918   56.49050
```


:::
:::

The most expensive candy types in the dataset were Nik L Nip, Nestle Smarties, Ring Pop, Hershey's Krackel, and Hershey's Milk Chocolate, with Nik L Nip being the least popular.

# 5.1 Optional
> Q21. Make a barplot again with geom_col() this time using pricepercent and then improve this step by step, first ordering the x-axis by value and finally making a so called “dot chat” or “lollipop” chart by swapping geom_col() for geom_point() + geom_segment().


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(pricepercent, reorder(rownames(candy), pricepercent)) +
  geom_col()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-26-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(candy) +
  aes(pricepercent, reorder(rownames(candy), pricepercent)) +
  geom_segment(aes(yend = reorder(rownames(candy), pricepercent), 
                   xend = 0), col="gray40") +
    geom_point()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-27-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


## 6 Exploring the correlation structure


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(corrplot)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
corrplot 0.95 loaded
```


:::

```{.r .cell-code}
cij <- cor(candy)
corrplot(cij)
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-28-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


> Q22. Examining this plot what two variables are anti-correlated (i.e. have minus values)?

fruity, chocolate
> Q23. Similarly, what two variables are most positively correlated?

chocolate, winpercent

## 7 PCA Principal Component Analysis

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pca <- prcomp(candy, scale=T)
summary(pca)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
Importance of components:
                          PC1    PC2    PC3     PC4    PC5     PC6     PC7
Standard deviation     2.0788 1.1378 1.1092 1.07533 0.9518 0.81923 0.81530
Proportion of Variance 0.3601 0.1079 0.1025 0.09636 0.0755 0.05593 0.05539
Cumulative Proportion  0.3601 0.4680 0.5705 0.66688 0.7424 0.79830 0.85369
                           PC8     PC9    PC10    PC11    PC12
Standard deviation     0.74530 0.67824 0.62349 0.43974 0.39760
Proportion of Variance 0.04629 0.03833 0.03239 0.01611 0.01317
Cumulative Proportion  0.89998 0.93832 0.97071 0.98683 1.00000
```


:::
:::

The main results figure: the PCA score plot:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(pca$x) +
  aes(PC1, PC2, label = rownames(pca$x)) +
  geom_point(col=my_cols) + 
  geom_text_repel(col=my_cols) + 
  labs(title="PCA Candy Space Map")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning: ggrepel: 54 unlabeled data points (too many overlaps). Consider
increasing max.overlaps
```


:::

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-30-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
my_data <- cbind(candy, pca$x[,1:3])
p <- ggplot(my_data) + 
        aes(x=PC1, y=PC2, 
            size=winpercent/100,  
            text=rownames(my_data),
            label=rownames(my_data)) +
        geom_point(col=my_cols)

p
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-31-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
#library(plotly)
#ggplotly(p)
```
:::


> Q24. Complete the code to generate the loadings plot above. What original variables are picked up strongly by PC1 in the positive direction? Do these make sense to you? Where did you see this relationship highlighted previously?

The "loadings" plot for PC1. The original variables in the positive direction are fruity, pluribus, and hard. These make sense because they have a correlation. This relationship was previously highlighted in the correlation plot. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(pca$rotation) +
  aes(PC1, reorder(rownames(pca$rotation),PC1)) +
  geom_col()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](Class_09_files/figure-pdf/unnamed-chunk-33-1.pdf){fig-pos='H'}
:::
:::


> Q25. Based on your exploratory analysis, correlation findings, and PCA results, what combination of characteristics appears to make a “winning” candy? How do these different analyses (visualization, correlation, PCA) support or complement each other in reaching this conclusion?

The winning candies came from chocolate candy bars. They all show the correlations of how different candies are similar to each other, and then how those candies that are similar to each other have similar winpercents over other candies.
